danger_signals.meaning: Loss of ball / game over condition

danger_signals.visual_form: Ball bounces off angled rails at edges
danger_signals.meaning: High-velocity trajectory change, risk of uncontrolled ball movement

safety_signals.visual_form: Horizontal paddle at bottom of play field

safety_signals.meaning: Ball deceleration and scoring opportunity zones

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ball rolling/bouncing on play field with paddle responsive to input
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Paddle tracks player input; ball physics consistent and predictable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Lady (Pauline) character sprite walking on platform above main play field
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Lady sprite moves independently; falling off edge triggers end state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ball absent from play field; score persists
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: New ball spawns at top or game transitions to next ball

environmental_storytelling.example: Lady (Pauline) character sprite appears in bonus round, referencing Donkey Kong arcade game; establishes narrative connection despite game being non-Mario title
Play field organized with bumpers, targets, and slingshots mimics real pinball table topology; guides player attention and ball flow

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Rolls downward due to gravity; bounces off bumpers and slingshots with variable trajectory
Moves left/right based on player input; contacts ball to redirect upward trajectory
Walks across platform; falls below paddle if no collision occurs; walking animation suggests panic/vulnerability

notes: Pinball (1985 NES) is a non-Mario digital adaptation of physical pinball mechanics. The game uses minimal narrative framing; world-state is communicated almost entirely through physics feedback and sprite animation. The inclusion of Pauline as a bonus game character provides the only explicit narrative connection to other Nintendo properties. Unused graphics suggest abandoned features: pop-up bumpers, one-way gates, and numeric rollover indicators (1-4) indicate developer iteration on table complexity. The game has no faction systems, political situations, or explicit danger/safety semantics—these emerge from pure mechanical interaction (ball loss = danger state; paddle positioning = safety state). Visual communication relies on position, velocity, and collision response rather than symbolic markers.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Pinball NES/FDS article)
